arabic: إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُمْ رَٰكِعُونَ
translation: “Your guardian is only God, His Messenger, and those who believe — who keep up prayer and give charity while bowing.”

sunni.label: Sunni emphasis
sunni.body: Most Sunni scholars read it as the believers as a whole — “while bowing” marks their humility and devotion, not a single successor.

shia.label: Shia emphasis
shia.body: Shia tradition reads it as Āyat al-Wilāyah — Imam ʿAlī gave his ring to a beggar while bowing, marking him the believers’ guardian.

voiceover.hook: How do two of Islam's great traditions read the same verse? Let's look.
voiceover.question: Which of these two readings resonates with you

cta_text: Which of these two readings resonates with you?
caption_desc: Āyat al-Wilāyah — one verse, two readings 🌙 #quran #tafsir #shia #sunni
